description: evtx-to-csv enrichment signature library. Edit and save, then click Refresh in the app.

patterns.name: ps_encoded_command
patterns.description: PowerShell launched with -EncodedCommand/-enc: Base64-encodes the payload to hide the actual command from casual inspection and bypass script-level logging. Standard in fileless malware, phishing lures, and post-exploitation frameworks.

patterns.name: ps_invoke_expression
patterns.description: Invoke-Expression (IEX) executes an arbitrary string as code at runtime. Commonly used to run remotely-fetched scripts without writing them to disk — a hallmark of fileless malware.

patterns.name: ps_download_cradle
patterns.description: Remote script download via WebClient/DownloadString or Invoke-WebRequest. Pulls attacker-controlled code directly into memory, bypassing traditional AV scanning.

patterns.name: ps_bits_transfer
patterns.description: BITS abused for file download/upload. BITS transfers survive reboots, run as a background service, and blend with legitimate Windows update traffic.

patterns.name: ps_execution_bypass
patterns.description: PowerShell execution policy bypass or hidden-window flags (-nop, -ep bypass, -WindowStyle Hidden). Used to suppress policy restrictions and avoid visible console windows.

patterns.name: ps_reflection_load
patterns.description: Reflective assembly loading injects a .NET DLL directly into memory without touching disk. Used to load offensive tools (Mimikatz, Rubeus) while bypassing file-based AV detection.

patterns.name: ps_remoting
patterns.description: PowerShell remoting used with a -ComputerName target — indicates lateral movement attempt via WinRM.

patterns.name: ps_network_socket
patterns.description: Direct use of .NET network socket classes in a script. Indicates a custom C2 channel or data exfiltration routine built without using standard web cmdlets.

patterns.name: ps_amsi_bypass
patterns.description: AMSI bypass attempt detected. Attackers patch amsi.dll in memory or manipulate the AMSI context to prevent real-time scanning of PowerShell/VBScript/JScript payloads.

patterns.name: lolbin_certutil_download
patterns.description: certutil.exe used to download a remote file — a well-known LOLBin abuse technique. Certutil is a trusted Windows binary, so downloads bypass many web-filtering tools.

patterns.name: lolbin_mshta
patterns.description: mshta.exe executing an HTA file or remote URL. MSHTA runs HTML Applications with full scripting privileges, commonly abused to execute attacker payloads.

patterns.name: lolbin_regsvr32_squiblydoo
patterns.description: Squiblydoo attack: regsvr32.exe used with /i:http and scrobj.dll to execute a remote COM scriptlet. Bypasses AppLocker and leaves minimal traces on disk.

patterns.name: lolbin_rundll32_minidump
patterns.description: rundll32.exe invoking comsvcs.dll MiniDump — used to dump LSASS process memory to extract credential hashes without requiring Mimikatz on disk.

patterns.name: lolbin_wmic_exec
patterns.description: WMIC used for process creation or remote execution — a classic LOLBin for execution without spawning cmd.exe directly.

patterns.name: lolbin_cscript_wscript
patterns.description: CScript/WScript executing a script file. Malware frequently uses these to run .vbs/.js droppers that evade macro-based detection.

patterns.name: lolbin_installutil
patterns.description: InstallUtil.exe abused to execute arbitrary .NET code. Bypasses AppLocker and application whitelisting by running under a trusted Microsoft binary.

patterns.name: lolbin_cmstp
patterns.description: CMSTP.exe exploited to execute arbitrary DLLs or COM scriptlets. Bypasses UAC and AppLocker on unpatched systems.

patterns.name: lolbin_msiexec_remote
patterns.description: msiexec.exe used to install a package from a remote URL — allows execution of arbitrary code without a visible installer UI.

patterns.name: lolbin_psexec
patterns.description: PsExec used for remote command execution. Frequently abused by ransomware operators for lateral movement across Windows environments.

patterns.name: defender_tamper
patterns.description: Windows Defender configuration modified via Set-MpPreference/Add-MpPreference. Adding exclusion paths or disabling real-time monitoring is a primary pre-ransomware step.

patterns.name: shadow_copy_delete
patterns.description: Shadow copy/backup deletion detected. Executed immediately before ransomware encryption to prevent victim recovery without paying.

patterns.name: event_log_clear_cmd
patterns.description: Windows event log cleared via wevtutil or PowerShell Clear-EventLog. Attackers clear logs to destroy forensic evidence after completing their objectives.

patterns.name: bcdedit_recovery_disabled
patterns.description: bcdedit used to disable Windows recovery. Prevents the OS from booting into recovery mode after ransomware encryption.

patterns.name: credential_dump_tool
patterns.description: Known credential dumping tool or technique detected: Mimikatz, WCE, fgdump, pwdump, gsecdump, or comsvcs MiniDump of LSASS.

patterns.name: credential_dump_lsass
patterns.description: Direct LSASS process dump detected. Dumping lsass.exe provides all logged-on user credential hashes and Kerberos tickets.

patterns.name: remote_access_tool
patterns.description: Unauthorized remote access tool detected (AnyDesk misuse, ngrok, netcat, socat, chisel, Ligolo, Meterpreter, or Cobalt Strike artefacts).

patterns.name: persistence_run_key
patterns.description: Registry Run/RunOnce key modification via reg add. Adding entries causes the specified program to execute at every user login.

patterns.name: persistence_schtask_create
patterns.description: Scheduled task created via schtasks /create. Malware uses scheduled tasks to survive reboots and re-execute at intervals.

patterns.name: persistence_service_create
patterns.description: Windows service created or reconfigured via sc create/config or New-Service. Attackers install malicious services for persistent SYSTEM-level execution.

patterns.name: recon_whoami
patterns.description: whoami executed — standard first recon step after initial access to confirm the compromised user account and its privilege level.

patterns.name: recon_net_user_domain
patterns.description: net user /domain or net group — domain user/group enumeration. Attackers run these to identify admin accounts before moving laterally.

patterns.name: recon_systeminfo
patterns.description: systeminfo executed — gathers OS version, patch level, hotfixes, and domain info. Used to identify unpatched CVEs.

patterns.name: recon_network_discovery
patterns.description: Network configuration discovery (arp, route, netstat, ipconfig). Reveals network topology and active connections for lateral movement planning.

patterns.name: recon_tasklist
patterns.description: tasklist or Get-Process used to enumerate running processes. Attackers use this to identify security tools and valuable targets like lsass.exe.

patterns.name: heuristic_base64_in_command
patterns.description: Long Base64 string detected in a command-line or script field. Strongly suggests obfuscated payload delivery.

patterns.name: heuristic_temp_path_execution
patterns.description: Executable running from a Temp, AppData, or Downloads path — common staging locations for malware that avoids writing to monitored system directories.

patterns.name: heuristic_double_extension
patterns.description: File with double extension detected (e.g. invoice.pdf.exe). A classic masquerading technique to trick users into treating an executable as a benign document.
